name: Required literals must be present in the URI

cases.name: Form-style query operator requires the leading `?`
cases.reason: The `?` operator unconditionally emits `?` as its first character when any variable is defined. A URI lacking `?` cannot be the round-trip result of any binding.

cases.name: Fragment operator requires the leading `#`
cases.reason: Fragment expansion always begins with `#` when the variable is defined. The only no-`#` alternative would have var undefined, which would round-trip to just `X`, not `Xvalue`.

cases.name: Path-style parameter requires the leading `;`
cases.reason: The `;` operator unconditionally emits `;` before the parameter. Without that prefix the URI cannot be a valid expansion.

cases.name: Trailing literal that is not present in the URI causes match to fail
cases.reason: The required trailing literal `/end` is not present anywhere in the URI; no slicing of `+path` can produce a valid round-trip.

cases.name: Surrounding literal at wrong position rejects the URI
cases.reason: After consuming `/api/` and the `{id}` expression, the tail must equal `/profile`. `/page` cannot match because simple expansion forbids `/` in `id`.

cases.name: Pct-encoded literal does not match its decoded form
cases.reason: Literal text in a template is matched verbatim. The literal `%2F` only matches the three-character sequence `%2F`, not the single byte `/`.

name: Operator allow-sets gate which characters can appear in the captured value

cases.name: Simple expansion does not permit a raw `/` in the captured value
cases.reason: Simple expansion percent-encodes `/`. A URI containing a raw `/` cannot be the round-trip result of any binding because expansion would emit `%2F`.

cases.name: Reserved expansion captures raw `/` as part of the value
cases.reason: Reserved expansion (`+`) allows reserved characters unencoded. The whole URI is the value of `path`.

cases.name: Reserved expansion preserves pct-encoded triplets verbatim
cases.reason: Per RFC 6570 §3.2.3, reserved expansion emits already-encoded triplets without re-encoding. Match must retain the encoded form rather than decode it to `a/b`.

cases.name: Fragment expansion treats already-encoded triplets verbatim
cases.reason: Like `+`, the `#` operator allows reserved + pct-encoding (U+R). The captured value retains its encoded form.

name: Prefix modifier must yield a binding consistent with the truncated value

cases.name: Prefix length equal to the value length is a no-op
cases.reason: A 5-char prefix on a 5-char value truncates to the original. Match binds the full string.

cases.name: Prefix length larger than the value is also a no-op
cases.reason: A prefix length larger than the value returns the full value unchanged. Match binds var to the literal URI content.

cases.name: URI longer than the declared prefix length cannot round-trip
cases.reason: Any binding for `var` would expand to its first 3 characters. The URI `value` has length 5, so no binding can round-trip.

cases.name: Prefix and full reference of the same variable must agree
cases.reason: First occurrence forces var[:1] === 'v'; second forces var === 'wrong'. truncate('wrong', 1) === 'w' ≠ 'v', so no binding satisfies both.

cases.name: Encoded prefix segment is consistent with the full encoded segment
cases.reason: semi=';'. truncate(';', 2) === ';'. Both occurrences expand to `%3B`, so the URI matches with semi=';'.

name: Boundary disambiguation across multiple literal occurrences

cases.name: Reserved expansion picks the boundary that lets the trailing literal match the URI tail
cases.reason: `/end` appears twice. Only when `+path` consumes through the first `/end` (binding `path` to `/a/b/end`) does the remaining tail equal the trailing literal.

cases.name: Simple expansion forbids `/`, eliminating all candidate boundaries
cases.reason: Simple expansion cannot capture a `/`. Both possible boundaries assign a value containing `/`, so no binding round-trips.

cases.name: Empty captured value between fixed literals is allowed when ifemp permits it
cases.reason: Simple expansion of an empty string emits nothing. Match recovers `empty` as the empty string.

name: Named operators: empty values, missing variables, and name mismatches

cases.name: Empty value with `=` separator binds the variable to empty string
cases.reason: Form-style query expansion of an empty string emits `name=` per the `ifemp` table entry. Match recovers `x` as the empty string.

cases.name: Path-style flag form (`;name` without `=`) binds the variable to empty string
cases.reason: The `;` operator with an empty value emits `;name` (no `=`) per `ifemp`. Match recovers `empty` as the empty string.

cases.name: Form-style with one of two variables undefined
cases.reason: Undefined variables are skipped during expansion. Match must allow the same: `x` is unbound, `y` is bound to `value`.

cases.name: Named query with a mismatched parameter name fails to match
cases.reason: The `?` operator emits the varname literally. `other` is not the varname `x`, so no binding can produce this URI.

cases.name: Named explode of a list emits the varname per element
cases.reason: Per RFC 6570 §3.2.8, an exploded list under `?` emits `list=red&list=green&...`. Repetition of the same name signals list semantics.

name: Composite values: list vs. associative disambiguation

cases.name: Exploded named pairs decode as an associative array
cases.reason: Each `name=value` part with a unique name decodes as an entry of the associative form. The list interpretation would require all parts to share `name === 'keys'`.

cases.name: Non-exploded composite under a path segment serializes as comma-separated key,value pairs
cases.reason: An even-numbered comma list pairs to a valid associative form whose round-trip equals the URI. The associative interpretation is preferred over list when both round-trip identically.

cases.name: Exploded path segments decode as a list
cases.reason: `{/list*}` distributes list elements as `/`-separated segments. Match recovers the list of three elements.

name: Decoding failures bubble up as match failure

cases.name: Invalid pct-encoded byte sequence cannot be decoded under simple expansion
cases.reason: decodeURIComponent('bad%FFseq') throws because `%FF` is not a valid UTF-8 byte. No binding can produce this URI under simple expansion (which percent-decodes captured values).

cases.name: Truncated pct-encoded triplet at end of value
cases.reason: `%2` is an incomplete pct-encoded triplet. decodeURIComponent throws, so no binding can round-trip under simple expansion.

cases.name: Reserved expansion accepts an otherwise-undecodable triplet verbatim
cases.reason: The same URI fails under simple expansion because decodeURIComponent rejects the `%FF` byte. Under reserved expansion, captured triplets are emitted verbatim — no decoding is performed — so the binding round-trips.

name: ActivityPub-style URIs combine host and resource paths

cases.name: Actor URI splits host and username across two simple expansions
cases.reason: Simple expansion captures `example.com` for `host` and `alice` for `user`. The intervening literal `/users/` fixes the boundary the first expression must stop at.

cases.name: Three-level actor object URI binds host, user, and object id
cases.reason: Each simple expansion is bounded by a surrounding literal. `-` is in the unreserved set, so `abc-123` round-trips for `id` without encoding.

cases.name: Reserved expansion captures host with port while trailing path remains literal
cases.reason: Simple expansion would percent-encode `:`. Reserved expansion (`+`) allows reserved characters unencoded, so `host` retains the port suffix verbatim.

cases.name: Pct-encoded UTF-8 username decodes to its Unicode form
cases.reason: Simple expansion percent-decodes the captured triplets as UTF-8. Re-expanding `페디파이` produces the same byte sequence, so the binding round-trips.

cases.name: WebFinger discovery URL captures host literal and form-style query parameter
cases.reason: Form-style expansion (`?`) percent-encodes `:` and `@`. The captured value decodes to `acct:alice@example.com`; expanding it re-encodes the same octets to round-trip identically.

cases.name: Mismatched trailing literal between expressions rejects the URI
cases.reason: After consuming `host` and `user`, the trailing literal `/inbox` does not appear in the URI. Simple expansion cannot capture `/`, so no boundary lets both literals align.

name: REST resource routes mix path segments and query parameters

cases.name: Versioned API path joins literal with two simple expansions and a path segment
cases.reason: `{version}` is bounded by the literal `/users`; `{/id}` consumes the final `/42`. Both values are unreserved, so they round-trip without encoding.

cases.name: Form-style query with two parameters captures both as separate bindings
cases.reason: The `?` operator emits `?page=2&limit=20`. Each named part decodes into its declared variable in declaration order.

cases.name: Path-style segment combined with explode list query
cases.reason: `{?include*}` distributes a list as repeated `include=…` parameters per RFC 6570 §3.2.8. Match recovers the list of two members; `{/id}` separately captures `42`.

cases.name: Exploded path segments yield a list across multiple URI segments
cases.reason: `{/path*}` distributes list members as `/`-separated segments. The string interpretation `path = "a/b/c"` would round-trip to `/a%2Fb%2Fc`, so only the list binding is consistent.

cases.name: Optional query expression is absent and matches with the variable unbound
cases.reason: When all variables in a `{?…}` expression are undefined, the expansion is the empty string. The URI ends right after `{/id}`, so `fields` remains unbound.

cases.name: Form-style query with an unrecognized parameter name fails to match
cases.reason: `{?fields}` requires the literal name `fields`. The named operator must consume every part of its expression body, so the unknown `wrong` part cannot be discarded.

name: Form-style query continuation appends parameters after a fixed query prefix

cases.name: Continuation operator binds two variables after a literal query prefix
cases.reason: The `&` operator emits `&x=1024&y=768` after the literal `?fixed=yes`. Each named part decodes into its declared variable in order.

cases.name: Form-style query feeds into a continuation across two expressions
cases.reason: `{?q}` emits `?q=cats`; `{&page}` emits `&page=2`. Match must split the URI tail at the start of `&page` so each expression sees only its own parts.

cases.name: Two consecutive continuation expressions split distinct parameters
cases.reason: Each `&…` expression declares exactly one named variable, so the only consistent split places `&q=alice` in the first expression and `&page=2` in the second.

cases.name: Continuation operator with a mismatched parameter name fails
cases.reason: `{&page}` requires the literal name `page`. The shortened `pg` cannot satisfy any binding under the named operator, so no boundary between the two expressions succeeds.

name: Fragment-anchored deep links carry path and fragment in separate expressions

cases.name: Path expression precedes fragment expression
cases.reason: Simple expansion would percent-encode `#`, so `{page}` cannot consume past the fragment delimiter. `{#anchor}` then absorbs the rest, including the leading `#`.

cases.name: Section and id captured as path segments before a fragment
cases.reason: Three expressions cooperate: `{section}` and `{id}` are simple expansions bounded by `/` literals; `{#anchor}` captures the fragment with the `#` prefix as its own first character.

cases.name: Missing fragment leaves the variable unbound
cases.reason: An undefined variable under `#` produces no output. `{#anchor}` matches the empty tail of the URI, leaving `anchor` unbound.

cases.name: Acct URI splits user and host around a literal `@`
cases.reason: The literal `@` between the two simple expansions disambiguates the boundary. Both captured values contain only unreserved characters, so they round-trip without encoding.

name: Repeated variable names across expressions must agree on a single value

cases.name: Same simple variable appearing twice with the same value succeeds
cases.reason: RFC 6570 §3.2.1 requires a variable's value to remain static throughout the expansion. Both occurrences capture `en`, so a single binding `lang = 'en'` satisfies both.

cases.name: Same simple variable appearing twice with conflicting values fails
cases.reason: The first occurrence forces `lang = 'en'` and the second forces `lang = 'ko'`. No single binding can satisfy both, so the merge step rejects the candidate.

cases.name: Same variable shared between simple and reserved expansions, both consistent
cases.reason: Simple expansion of `notes` is `notes`; reserved expansion of `notes` is also `notes`. Both occurrences agree on `path = 'notes'`.

cases.name: Same variable shared between simple and reserved expansions diverges on encoding
cases.reason: `{path}` decodes `foo%2Fbar` to `foo/bar`; `{+path}` captures `foo/bar` verbatim. Both occurrences resolve to the same `path = 'foo/bar'`, and re-expanding under each operator reproduces the URI.

cases.name: Same variable shared between simple and reserved expansions with mismatched payloads fails
cases.reason: Simple expansion forces `path = 'foo'`; reserved expansion forces `path = 'bar'`. The two bindings cannot be reconciled, so the URI does not round-trip under any single context.

cases.name: Variable repeated across path and form-style query with the same value
cases.reason: The first `{id}` consumes `42` from the path; the second emits `?id=42` under the `?` operator. Both occurrences agree, so a single binding `id = '42'` satisfies the whole template.

cases.name: Variable repeated across path and query diverges on the captured value
cases.reason: The path occurrence forces `id = '42'` and the query occurrence forces `id = '99'`. RFC 6570 §3.2.1 forbids the variable from taking two values within the same expansion.

cases.name: Prefix and full reference of the same variable both contribute to the binding
cases.reason: `{slug:3}` truncates `slug` to its first three characters (`int`); `{slug}` emits the full value. The full-value occurrence determines `slug = 'intro'`, and `truncate('intro', 3) === 'int'` confirms the prefix occurrence.

cases.name: Prefix and full reference of the same variable disagree on the truncation
cases.reason: Full reference forces `slug = 'intro'`, but `truncate('intro', 3)` is `int`, not `abc`. The prefix and full bindings cannot agree, so no candidate round-trips.
